title: Serverless PostgreSQL (Neon)
description: Serverless PostgreSQL with autoscaling compute and scale-to-zero. Key differentiator: database branching creates isolated copy-on-write database clones per branch or pull request, enabling safe schema migrations and preview environments without separate database instances.

provides.reasoning: Neon provides a fully-managed PostgreSQL database with ACID guarantees, standard SQL support, and all PostgreSQL extensions.
Neon automatically scales compute units up and down based on workload demand, including scale-to-zero during inactivity.
Neon's defining differentiator: create instant copy-on-write database branches for each git branch or PR, enabling isolated dev/preview environments without data duplication costs.
Neon ships with built-in PgBouncer connection pooling, enabling serverless functions to maintain thousands of concurrent connections without overwhelming PostgreSQL.
Neon stores all data changes as a WAL-based history, enabling point-in-time recovery to any moment within the retention window (7 days on free tier, up to 30 days on paid).
Neon is a fully managed serverless database service with no infrastructure to operate, automatic backups, and managed upgrades.
Neon is fully PostgreSQL-compatible; native RLS via CREATE POLICY is available without any additional configuration.

requires.reasoning: As a SaaS platform, Neon requires internet connectivity to access its hosted PostgreSQL service.
Applications must use a PostgreSQL-compatible client driver to connect to Neon; any language with a standard PostgreSQL driver is compatible.

connection_pooling.description: Enable built-in PgBouncer connection pooling. Essential for serverless/edge functions that open many short-lived connections. Disable only for applications that require full PostgreSQL session semantics (e.g., advisory locks, prepared transactions).

autoscaling.description: Enable automatic compute scaling based on workload. Neon adjusts CPU/memory between min and max compute units. Disable to pin to a fixed compute size for predictable latency.

scale_to_zero.description: Scale compute to zero after inactivity. Reduces costs dramatically but adds cold start latency (1-3s). Enable for dev/staging; consider disabling for production if latency SLAs are strict.

min_compute_units.description: Minimum compute units (CUs) when autoscaling. 0.25 CU = 0.25 vCPU / 1 GiB RAM. Higher minimum reduces cold-start risk but increases baseline cost.

max_compute_units.description: Maximum compute units for autoscaling. Caps the highest compute tier Neon can scale to. Set based on peak expected concurrency and query complexity.

database_branching.description: Enable Neon branching for isolated copy-on-write DB copies per git branch or PR. Excellent for preview environments and safely testing schema migrations before production. Disable in production environments where branches are not needed.

ssl_mode.description: Neon enforces SSL on all connections. 'require' is the default; 'verify-full' adds server certificate validation for maximum security.

cost.costNotes: Free tier: 0.5 GiB storage, 190 compute hours/month, 10 branches. Paid plans (Launch $19/month, Scale $69/month) unlock more storage, compute hours, and features. At $200/month you can run a well-provisioned production database with moderate traffic.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Neon free tier is $0/month covering 0.5 GiB storage and 190 compute hours. Launch plan ($19/month) adds 10 GiB storage and 300 compute hours. Scale plan ($69/month) includes 50 GiB storage and 750 compute hours. A production workload with moderate traffic (1-4 compute units, ~100 GiB storage, connection pooling) would typically run $50-$200/month. The max of $200 represents a typical production deployment; high-traffic applications may exceed this.
provenance.adoptionCost: Low complexity adoption. Neon is standard PostgreSQL, so any developer familiar with SQL and PostgreSQL drivers can adopt it quickly. Integration involves: creating a Neon project (5 mins), connecting via standard PostgreSQL connection string (15 mins), configuring connection pooling for serverless (1-2 hours), setting up database branching for CI/CD if desired (2-4 hours). Total ~8-12 hours at $50/hour with AI assistance. The main learning curve is understanding serverless-specific patterns (connection pooling, cold starts) rather than any Neon-specific API.
provenance.licenseCost: Neon is built on open-source PostgreSQL (PostgreSQL License) with the Neon serverless layer also open-sourced. The hosted service charges only for compute and storage consumed. No upfront license fees, no per-seat licensing.

availability.reason: Neon is publicly available with a generous free tier

supports_nfr.reason: Neon provides 99.99% availability SLA on paid plans. Free tier has lower effective availability (~99%) due to shared infrastructure and no SLA guarantee.
Serverless PostgreSQL with connection pooling typically achieves p95 latency of ~5ms for simple queries on warm compute; complex queries or first-connection overhead will be higher.
p99 latency accounts for tail latency from complex queries, connection setup, and occasional autoscaling events. Scale-to-zero cold starts (1-3s) are not included here but warned separately.
Neon's storage-compute separation enables fast failover; RTO is approximately 1 minute for automated recovery from compute failures.
Neon with autoscaling up to 10 CUs supports tens of thousands of read QPS with proper indexing and connection pooling.
Write throughput is bounded by WAL processing and storage I/O; 20K write QPS is achievable on larger compute tiers.
PostgreSQL audit logging (pgaudit extension) is available on Neon; optional based on compliance requirements.
Neon supports all PostgreSQL isolation patterns plus per-tenant database branching as an isolation model.
PostgreSQL with encryption at rest and in transit supports PII handling on paid plans. Free tier PII should be avoided due to lack of compliance SLA.
Neon supports GDPR compliance on paid plans with data residency options. Free tier lacks formal compliance guarantees.
CCPA compliance is achievable through PostgreSQL's data access and deletion capabilities on paid plans.
Neon does not offer a HIPAA BAA on any tier; HIPAA-regulated workloads should use a provider with a signed BAA.
SOX compliance requires long-term audit trails and access control guarantees that Neon does not formally certify.
Standard PostgreSQL provides read-your-writes consistency for single-region deployments within the same connection.
Neon's WAL-based storage provides strict durability on paid plans; free tier has best-effort durability with 7-day PITR.

supports_constraints.reason: Neon infrastructure runs on AWS; accessible from any cloud, on-prem, or local environment via standard PostgreSQL connection.
Neon is accessed via standard PostgreSQL drivers available for virtually every programming language.
Neon works with any platform that can open a TCP connection to PostgreSQL; especially well-suited for serverless/edge functions via its built-in connection pooler.
Pattern specifically requires Neon as the serverless PostgreSQL provider.
Neon is designed for OLTP workloads with transactional PostgreSQL semantics; it is the primary use case.
Neon is not designed for OLAP analytical workloads; use a dedicated warehouse (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift) for analytics.
PostgreSQL can store time-series data and Neon supports TimescaleDB extension; acceptable for moderate time-series workloads.
Neon supports the pgvector extension for vector similarity search and AI embeddings alongside relational data.
PostgreSQL JSONB columns support document-oriented data patterns alongside relational tables.
PostgreSQL can serve simple key-value access patterns via indexed lookup, but a dedicated KV store is more efficient at scale.
Neon/PostgreSQL is not a native graph database; use a dedicated graph DB (Neo4j, Neptune) for complex relationship traversal.
Neon can be used with or without an external caching layer depending on latency and throughput requirements.
Neon works with both synchronous and asynchronous application patterns.
Neon supports multi-tenancy through PostgreSQL RLS, schema-per-tenant, or database branching for per-tenant isolation.
Neon handles batch workloads via standard PostgreSQL; scale-to-zero may add latency for infrequent batch jobs.
Neon is a single-region PostgreSQL instance; distributed transactions across services are not natively supported.
PostgreSQL logical replication and WAL streaming enable real-time event patterns, though dedicated streaming platforms are more appropriate for high-volume event streaming.
Neon can store AI model metadata, embeddings (pgvector), and inference results but does not itself perform inference.

warn_nfr.reason: Neon free tier only provides daily backups (~24h RPO); achieving RPO < 60 minutes requires the Neon Launch plan (~$19/month) or above for Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR).
warn_nfr.message: db-managed-postgres--neon: RPO target is {actual} minutes (<60min). Near-zero RPO via PITR requires Neon Launch plan (~$19/month) or above. Free tier has ~24h RPO via daily snapshots only — confirm your Neon plan supports the required RPO.

warn_nfr.reason: scale_to_zero causes PostgreSQL cold starts of 1-3 seconds on first connection after inactivity. If p99 < 100ms is required, scale_to_zero should be disabled in production.
warn_nfr.message: db-managed-postgres--neon: p99 latency target is {actual}ms (<100ms). If scale_to_zero is enabled, cold starts (1-3s) will violate this target. Set scale_to_zero=false in defaultConfig for production.

warn_nfr.reason: Neon write throughput is bounded by WAL I/O on serverless compute. Above 15K write QPS, consider dedicated PostgreSQL (RDS, Cloud SQL) or a distributed database.
warn_nfr.message: db-managed-postgres--neon: peak write QPS is {actual} req/s (>15000). Neon serverless may hit WAL throughput limits at this scale; evaluate dedicated PostgreSQL or distributed DB.
